prior.title: Marine Fuel Decarbonisation Pathways
prior.summary: LNG, methanol, biofuels, wind-assist, and nuclear propulsion technologies for maritime decarbonisation. LNG is transitional (~20% GHG reduction). Owner: Sustainability + Operations.
prior.what_is_it: LNG, methanol, biofuels, wind-assist, and nuclear propulsion technologies for maritime decarbonisation. LNG is transitional (~20% GHG reduction). Green methanol vessels entering service (Maersk). Wind-assist retrofits showing 5-30% fuel savings.
prior.why_matters: Carrier fleet composition determines ETS and FuelEU cost exposure. Operators who understand which fuels and technologies reduce compliance costs can select carriers with lower surcharge trajectories.
key_data: LNG: ~20% GHG reduction, methane slip concerns
Green methanol: first vessels in service (Maersk), limited supply
Wind-assist: 5-30% fuel savings on suitable routes
Nuclear: SMR concepts in development, not commercially available
prior.full_brief:
# Marine Fuel Decarbonisation Pathways

## Technology Profile

---

## 1. What's Being Tested or Deployed and By Whom

### IMO Regulatory Framework — Adopted and In Force

FACT: IMO Member States, meeting at the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC 80), adopted the 2023 IMO Strategy on Reduction of GHG Emissions from Ships, with enhanced targets to tackle harmful emissions.
*Source: Revised GHG Reduction Strategy for Global Shipping Adopted, IMO Press Briefing, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

FACT: The revised IMO GHG Strategy includes an enhanced common ambition to reach net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping by or around, i.e. close to, 2050, a commitment to ensure an uptake of alternative zero and near-zero GHG fuels by 2030, as well as indicative check-points for 2030 and 2040.
*Source: Revised GHG Reduction Strategy for Global Shipping Adopted, IMO Press Briefing, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

FACT: Indicative checkpoints require reduction of the total annual GHG emissions from international shipping by at least 20%, striving for 30%, by 2030, compared to 2008; and to reduce the total annual GHG emissions from international shipping by at least 70%, striving for 80%, by 2040, compared to 2008.
*Source: Revised GHG Reduction Strategy for Global Shipping Adopted, IMO Press Briefing, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

FACT: The Strategy sets uptake of zero or near-zero GHG emission technologies, fuels and/or energy sources to represent at least 5%, striving for 10%, of the energy used by international shipping by 2030.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

FACT: On 1 January 2023 it became mandatory for all ships to calculate their Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index (EEXI) and to establish their annual operational carbon intensity indicator (CII) and CII rating.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

---

### IMO Net-Zero Framework — Legally Binding Measures: Current Phase

FACT: IMO's Marine Environment Protection Committee 83rd session (MEPC 83) finalized a draft legal text for these measures, referred to as the "IMO Net-Zero Framework," in April 2025. This consists of amendments to Annex VI of the MARPOL Convention.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

FACT: These amendments were submitted with a view to adoption as legally binding measures in October 2025, during an extraordinary session of the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC/ES.2). The MEPC/ES.2 agreed to adjourn the meeting for one year until 2026 to consider the adoption of draft amendments to MARPOL Annex VI, including the IMO Net-Zero Framework.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

FACT: The next step is MEPC 84 (Spring 2026).
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

*Analytical inference:* The adjournment of MEPC/ES.2 represents a material delay in binding adoption. The Net-Zero Framework remains in draft form; entry into force is no earlier than 2027, contingent on adoption at a future extraordinary session or at MEPC 84/85. Workspaces operating ocean freight across global trade lanes should treat the current 2026–2027 window as the last planning interval before binding fuel-standard obligations become fixed.

---

### The Dual Mid-Term Measure Basket — Design and Intent

FACT: The 2023 GHG Strategy states that a basket of candidate measure(s), delivering on the reduction targets, should be developed and finalized comprised of both a technical element, namely a goal-based marine fuel standard regulating the phased reduction of the marine fuel's GHG intensity; and an economic element, on the basis of a maritime GHG emissions pricing mechanism.
*Source: Revised GHG Reduction Strategy for Global Shipping Adopted, IMO Press Briefing, 7 July 2023. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/pressbriefings/pages/revised-ghg-reduction-strategy-for-global-shipping-adopted-.aspx

*Analytical inference:* The economic element — a maritime GHG pricing mechanism — is the instrument with the most direct cost pass-through to ocean freight buyers. For workspaces procuring ocean freight on spot or annual contract terms, the pricing mechanism, once adopted, will convert from a regulatory signal into a line-item surcharge. The mechanism's design (levy vs. cap-and-trade, pass-through structure, flag-state allocation) is not yet settled and determines whether the workspace absorbs cost or passes it through in freight pricing.

---

### FuelEU Maritime — In Force from January 2025

FACT: The FuelEU maritime regulation (Regulation (EU) 2023/1805) is fully applied from 1 January 2025 except for articles 8 and 9 on monitoring plans that are applied from August 2024.
*Source: Decarbonising Maritime Transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission, https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

FACT: FuelEU maritime sets maximum limits for the yearly average greenhouse gas (GHG) intensity of the energy used by ships above 5,000 gross tonnage calling at European ports, regardless of their flag. Targets will ensure that the greenhouse gas intensity of fuels used in the sector will gradually decrease over time, starting with a 2% decrease by 2025 and reaching up to an 80% reduction by 2050.
*Source: Decarbonising Maritime Transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission, https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

FACT: The targets cover not only CO₂ but also methane and nitrous oxide emissions over the full lifecycle of the fuels used onboard, on a well-to-wake (WtW) basis.
*Source: Decarbonising Maritime Transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission, https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

FACT: Annual average carbon intensity reduction compared to the average in 2020: 2025: -2%; 2030: -6%; 2035: -14.5%; 2040: -31%; 2045: -62%; 2050: -80%.
*Source: Decarbonising Maritime Transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission, https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

FACT: Passenger and container ships at berth or moored at the quayside must use on-shore power supply (OPS) or alternative zero-emission technologies from 1 January 2030 onwards in ports covered under article 9 of the alternative fuels infrastructure regulation (AFIR), and from 1 January 2035 in all EU ports that develop OPS capacity.
*Source: Decarbonising Maritime Transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission, https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

*Analytical inference:* FuelEU Maritime's 2030 OPS mandate for passenger and container vessels at EU ports is the near-term inflection point most visible to ocean freight buyers. Workspaces routing shipments via EU-calling container vessels will begin seeing FuelEU compliance costs reflected in carrier surcharges from 2025 onward, escalating materially at the -6% threshold in 2030.

---

### Alternative Fuel Candidates — Projected Market Trajectory

FACT: E-ammonia is expected to capture an average of 35% of the global marine fuel mix by 2050. When combined with blue ammonia, adoption could range between 20–60%, with demand projected to grow from 0.79 EJ in 2030 to 6.06 EJ in 2050.
*Source: The Future of Maritime Fuels, Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub, 11 September 2023. https://www.thedecarbhub.org/insights-and-resources/the-future-of-maritime-fuels/

FACT: Biofuels, particularly liquefied bio-methane, could account for around 34% of the fuel mix by 2050. However, supply is unlikely to keep pace with demand, with forecasts of only 0.3–2 EJ available compared to 4.58 EJ required.
*Source: The Future of Maritime Fuels, Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub, 11 September 2023. https://www.thedecarbhub.org/insights-and-resources/the-future-of-maritime-fuels/

FACT: Despite rising interest in methanol-fuelled vessels, methanol is projected to make up only ~13.4% of demand by 2050. Supply constraints are expected to limit its role in the long-term fuel mix.
*Source: The Future of Maritime Fuels, Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub, 11 September 2023. https://www.thedecarbhub.org/insights-and-resources/the-future-of-maritime-fuels/

*Analytical inference:* The bio-methane supply gap — 0.3–2 EJ available against 4.58 EJ required by 2050 — signals that near-term carrier claims of biofuel use will face increasing credibility and availability scrutiny. Workspaces relying on carrier biofuel certificates for Scope 3 reporting should treat bio-methane availability as structurally constrained and validate sourcing claims before using them in client-facing sustainability disclosures.

---

### Scale of Transition Required: Quantitative Context

FACT: The study finds that 15–26% alternative fuel penetration is needed by 2030, well beyond IMO's 5–10% target, with WtW lifecycle framing.
*Source: Technical Requirements for 2023 IMO GHG Strategy, MDPI Sustainability, March 2024. https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/16/7/2766

*Analytical inference:* The peer-reviewed finding that 15–26% alternative fuel penetration is required to meet the 2030 carbon intensity goals — versus the IMO's stated 5–10% ambition level — implies a significant compliance gap in the mid-term. Workspaces should not assume that carrier fleets will be fully compliant with the spirit of the 2030 targets simply because they are technically within the IMO's minimum threshold. Independent verification of carrier decarbonisation progress will become a due-diligence requirement in client sustainability reporting.

---

## 2. What This Tells Us About Industry Trajectory

*Analytical inference:* The convergence of four independent signals — the IMO 2023 Strategy's binding checkpoints, FuelEU Maritime already in force, the LR Decarb Hub's fuel mix projections, and the MDPI quantitative gap analysis — points to an industry transition that is underway but structurally underfunded and supply-constrained. The direction is unambiguous; the pace and the winning fuel are not.

*Analytical inference:* Three drivers are accelerating conversion from signal to commercial reality: (1) FuelEU Maritime is already imposing costs on EU-port-calling vessels from 2025, creating an immediate carrier cost that will be passed through in freight rates; (2) the IMO Net-Zero Framework, once adopted, will extend equivalent pressure globally; (3) bio-methane supply constraints mean that early-mover carriers locking in alternative fuel supply contracts will gain competitive separation from those relying on spot availability.

*Analytical inference:* The trajectory strongly favours ammonia as the dominant long-term fuel, with methanol filling a near-term transition role due to fleet compatibility. Workspaces with ocean freight on long-haul routes (Americas–Europe, Asia–Europe) are positioned to face the largest fuel transition surcharges in the 2028–2032 window as carriers begin operating dual-fuel vessels and recouping capital costs.

No single operator has been confirmed in the provided source blocks as having committed to a specific ammonia-fuelled commercial fleet deployment. The LR Decarb Hub projection is a scenario analysis, not a deployment report.

*Source: The Future of Maritime Fuels, Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub, 11 September 2023. https://www.thedecarbhub.org/insights-and-resources/the-future-of-maritime-fuels/

---

## 3. Supplier Access and Procurement Reality

SEVERITY: MONITORING

*Analytical inference:* The LR Decarb Hub scenario analysis identifies e-ammonia and blue ammonia as the leading long-term fuel candidates, but the analysis frames adoption as dependent on supply chain development and cross-sector investment alignment. No specific commercial availability dates for ammonia bunkering at scale are confirmed in the source blocks provided.

*Specific commercial bunkering availability dates for e-ammonia and bio-methane at named global ports not available from primary sources as of 2025.*

FACT: The Future of Maritime Fuels report stresses that sector-wide leadership, cross-sector collaboration, and initiatives such as green corridors are vital to de-risk adoption and scale up sustainable fuels.
*Source: The Future of Maritime Fuels, Lloyd's Register Maritime Decarbonisation Hub, 11 September 2023. https://www.thedecarbhub.org/insights-and-resources/the-future-of-maritime-fuels/

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces procuring ocean freight, supplier access to decarbonised fuel is currently mediated entirely through the carrier. The workspace does not purchase marine fuel. What the workspace can negotiate is access to green freight products — carrier-offered instruments (e.g., book-and-claim, mass-balance biofuel certificates, SAF/green methanol certificates) — that allow emissions attribution without physical fuel delivery. The reliability of these instruments depends on the carrier's underlying fuel sourcing, which is currently not independently auditable by the shipper.

*Analytical inference:* FuelEU Maritime's well-to-wake (WtW) accounting methodology, now in force, creates a verifiable compliance record at the carrier level for EU port calls. Workspaces sourcing carrier sustainability claims for Scope 3 reporting should request FuelEU compliance documentation (monitoring plans, verified reports) rather than relying solely on carrier marketing materials.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* whether carrier-provided FuelEU compliance data (monitoring plans and verified annual reports under Regulation (EU) 2023/1805) constitutes sufficient evidence for Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) reporting under CSRD/ESRS or GHG Protocol standards requires confirmation from the workspace's sustainability reporting counsel.

---

## 4. Operational Fit by Transport Mode and Cargo Vertical

### Ocean — Primary Exposure (Tertiary Mode, High Freight-Cost Sensitivity)

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces routing ocean freight — across Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes — face direct exposure to FuelEU Maritime cost pass-through on EU-port-calling vessels from 2025, with step-change cost increases at the -6% GHG intensity threshold in 2030.

FACT: FuelEU maritime sets maximum limits for the yearly average greenhouse gas (GHG) intensity of the energy used by ships above 5,000 gross tonnage calling at European ports, regardless of their flag.
*Source: Decarbonising Maritime Transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission, https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

*Operational implication:* For shipments of high-value cargo (live events equipment, fine art, luxury goods, high-value automotive components) that move by ocean on cost or space grounds, the FuelEU compliance cost trajectory — 2% reduction in 2025 rising to 80% by 2050 — will add an escalating premium to ocean freight rates on EU routes. The magnitude of the per-unit cost impact depends on the freight rate base and the carrier's fuel mix; effect on individual cargo verticals requires carrier-specific data.

*Operational implication:* Humanitarian cargo routed by ocean via EU ports is not categorically exempt from FuelEU Maritime's scope. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific humanitarian shipment categories qualify for any exemption under Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 Articles 2(3) or 2(6) (the provisions under which Member State exemptions have been granted to Greece, Italy, Denmark, Malta, Spain, France, and Portugal) requires confirmation from the workspace's legal counsel reviewing the applicable exemption decisions.

### Air — Primary Mode

FACT: The 2023 IMO GHG Strategy applies to international shipping. Air transport is outside the IMO framework.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

*Analytical inference:* Marine fuel decarbonisation pathways do not directly affect air freight operations. However, workspaces whose clients require end-to-end supply chain GHG reporting (Scope 3, CSRD, or contractual sustainability KPIs) will need to manage marine and air emissions accounting separately, using different methodologies (WtW for marine under FuelEU; CORSIA/ICAO for aviation). Conflating or aggregating these without methodology disclosure creates material risk in external reporting.

### Road — Secondary Mode

*Analytical inference:* Road freight operations are not within the scope of IMO or FuelEU Maritime. However, drayage and road legs connecting port to warehouse are part of the same multimodal journey and subject to separate EU road fleet CO₂ standards. The marine decarbonisation cost chain does not end at the port gate; workspaces managing port-to-destination legs should track road freight emissions separately.

---

## 5. Competitive Positioning Implications for the Workspace

SEVERITY: COMPETITIVE EDGE

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that can credibly track, document, and report carrier GHG intensity data at the shipment level — using FuelEU-verified carrier reports and IMO DCS data — will be positioned to offer clients verifiable Scope 3 ocean freight emissions accounts. This is a near-term differentiator as CSRD reporting obligations cascade to mid-market shippers from 2026–2028.

*Analytical inference:* The bio-methane supply gap identified by the LR Decarb Hub (0.3–2 EJ available versus 4.58 EJ required by 2050) creates a credibility risk for competitors who over-claim green freight credentials based on unverified biofuel certificates. Workspaces that apply WtW methodology and request FuelEU-verified data from carriers will be able to distinguish genuine decarbonisation from certificate-based greenwashing in client reporting.

Named competitor positioning: *Specific named competitor fleet commitments or green freight product positions are not confirmed in the source blocks provided. No named competitor data is emitted.*

*Analytical inference:* Carriers that have ordered methanol-capable or ammonia-capable newbuilds (publicly reported in trade media but not confirmed in the source blocks provided) will be positioned to offer lower-intensity freight products from the late 2020s. Workspaces that establish preferred carrier relationships with these operators before the vessels enter service will have access to lower-intensity freight at launch rather than competing for capacity after launch.

---

## 6. Conversational and Strategic Talking Points

### What the workspace can credibly say to clients

- The IMO's 2023 Strategy sets a net-zero target for international shipping by 2050, with a 20–30% GHG reduction checkpoint by 2030. This is binding at the strategic level; the specific legal instruments (the Net-Zero Framework) are in draft, with adoption expected in 2026 following a delay from the original October 2025 extraordinary MEPC session.
- FuelEU Maritime is already in force from 1 January 2025 for vessels above 5,000 gross tonnes calling at EU ports. This regulation covers the full well-to-wake lifecycle of marine fuels and will drive escalating fuel cost premiums on EU trade lanes.
- Alternative fuel projections — e-ammonia at ~35% of the marine fuel mix by 2050, bio-methane at ~34% but supply-constrained — are scenario analyses, not deployment confirmations. The workspace can reference these as forward indicators, not as guaranteed outcomes.
- The workspace can offer clients shipment-level GHG intensity documentation grounded in carrier FuelEU compliance data, which is verifiable under EU regulation.

### What to avoid saying

- Do not claim that any specific alternative fuel is "the solution" — the LR Decarb Hub explicitly identifies uncertainty and recommends diversified strategies.
- Do not cite the MDPI 15–26% alternative fuel penetration finding as an IMO requirement — it is a peer-reviewed quantitative analysis of what is needed, not a regulatory mandate.
- Do not confirm specific surcharge figures for FuelEU compliance costs — these are carrier-specific and not confirmed in the provided source material.
- Do not present bio-methane certificates as equivalent to physical low-emission fuel use without verifying the carrier's underlying sourcing against FuelEU monitoring records.

### Questions to pose to clients

- Does your Scope 3 reporting framework require well-to-wake (WtW) or tank-to-wake (TtW) methodology for ocean freight emissions? (FuelEU uses WtW; older frameworks may use TtW, creating a methodology mismatch.)
- Are your ocean carrier contracts structured to allow transparency on FuelEU annual compliance reports?
- Do your sustainability KPIs differentiate between physical fuel use and book-and-claim certificate instruments?

---

## 7. Time-to-Market, Procurement Window, and Action

SEVERITY: MONITORING — re-check at MEPC 84 (Spring 2026) for adoption status of the Net-Zero Framework.

### Confirmed milestones with sourced dates

FACT: MEPC 84 (Spring 2026) — next scheduled session after MEPC/ES.2 adjournment.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

FACT: MEPC 85 (Autumn 2026) — target date in the IMO timeline.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

FACT: MEPC 86 (Summer 2027) — Initiate the review of the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

FACT: MEPC 88 (Autumn 2028) — Finalization of the review of the 2023 IMO GHG Strategy with a view to adoption of the 2028 IMO Strategy on reduction of GHG emissions from ships.
*Source: IMO's work to cut GHG emissions from ships, IMO Hot Topics, https://imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx

*Analytical inference:* If the Net-Zero Framework is adopted at MEPC 84 (Spring 2026) or at a subsequent extraordinary session, entry into force would follow approximately 16 months after adoption per the IMO's own indicated timeline (citing "16 months after adoption of measures (2027)" in the published schedule). Workspaces should treat 2027 as the earliest plausible date for legally binding global marine fuel standards to enter force.

### Actions — specific and sequenced

1. **Now (Q3 2025 – Q1 2026): Carrier data audit.** Request FuelEU monitoring plan and first verified annual report from each ocean carrier used on EU-port-calling trade lanes. Establish what fuel intensity data is available at the voyage level. This is the baseline for any client-facing GHG reporting.

2. **Before MEPC 84 (Spring 2026): Contract clause review.** *Legal Confirmation Required:* review ocean freight contracts for GHG surcharge pass-through language and whether FuelEU compliance costs are expressly allocated or left to carrier discretion. This window closes when the Net-Zero Framework is adopted and carriers begin pricing the new global pricing mechanism into contract negotiations.

3. **Q1 2026: Internal Scope 3 methodology alignment.** Confirm that the workspace's Scope 3 Category 4 methodology uses WtW accounting consistent with FuelEU Maritime, to avoid restatement risk when CSRD/ESRS reporting cascades.

4. **Q2 2026 (post-MEPC 84): Reassess based on Net-Zero Framework adoption status.** If adopted, initiate commercial conversations with ocean carriers about green freight product access under the new pricing mechanism. If not adopted, extend monitoring to MEPC 85 (Autumn 2026).

5. **Ongoing: Bio-methane certificate scrutiny.** For any carrier-provided biofuel instruments used in Scope 3 reporting, document the sourcing chain and verify against FuelEU annual reports. The supply gap identified by LR Decarb Hub makes unverified bio-methane certificates a reputational and reporting risk.

---
